coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of N, P-doped carbon quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ and scale inhibition
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shu-Rong
Cai, Shao-Kang
Wang, Gui-Qiao
Cui, Jin-Zhi
Gao, Can-Zhu
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen, phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) were synthesized by a low-cost and simple one-step hydrothermal method by using Amino trimethylene phosphonic acid (ATMP) and sodium citrate as the nitrogen source, phosphorus source and carbon source. The synthesized N, P-CQDs had bright blue fluorescence, good water dispersibility and stability. The fluorescence of N, P-CQDs was only quenched by Fe2+ and Fe3+, indicating extremely high selectivity to Fe2+ and Fe3+. The N, P-CQDs fluorescent probe could specifically measure Fe2+ and Fe3+ in the range of 0-600 μM and 0-250 μM and the limits of detection were 0.298 μM and 0.447 μM, respectively. The sensor system based on N, P-CQDs could also be used to determine Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in real water samples. Through research, it was found that the quenching mechanism of Fe2+/Fe3+ on N, P-CQDs may be static quenching. Besides, N, P-CQDs showed excellent CaCO3 scale inhibition efficiency. Therefore, synthesized N, P-CQDs has broad application prospects in iron ions detection and scale inhibition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nitrogen, phosphorus-doped
Fluorescence quenching
Fe2+ detection
Fe3+ detection
Scale inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of metal ions by N, P-CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for synthesizing N, P-CQDs and their application in Fe2+, Fe3+ detection and scale inhibition.
Morphology characterization of N, P-CQD. (a) TEM images, (b) The particle size distribution results, (c) AFM images, inset: the height profile along the line in (c), (d) XRD pattern of N, P-CQDs.
(a) C1s XPS, (b) N1s XPS and (c) P2p XPS of the N, P-CQDs, (d) FTIR spectra of N, P-CQDs and the reaction precursors (Top: N, P-CQDs, below: reaction precursors).

(a) UV-vis absorbance (black), excitation (red), emission (blue) spectra of N, P-CQDs, inset: photographs of the aqueous dispersion of N, P-CQDs under sunlight and UV lamp (365 nm), (b) fluorescence emission spectra of N, P-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths, (c) effect of pH on the emission intensity of N, P-CQDs, (d) effect of ion concentration (K+) on the emission intensity of N, P-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of N, P-CQDs solution (PBS buffer at pH=7.2) in the presence of 100 μM different metal ions, (b) luminous color of N, P-CQDs solution containing different metal ions under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation.
Emission spectra of N, P-CQDs solution upon adding different concentrations of (a) Fe2+ and (c) Fe3+ (λex=320 nm), the relationship between F0/F and (b) Fe2+ and (d) Fe3+.

The quenching ratio of N, P-CQDs at different temperature, (a) with Fe2+, (b) with Fe3+, (c) UV-vis absorbance spectra of N, P-CQDs solution without or with Fe2+/Fe3+, (d) Fluorescence decay traces of N, P-CQDs solution without or with Fe2+/Fe3+.
Detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in real sample by N, P-CQDs

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a The average value of the five-time measured sample

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of N, P-doped carbon quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ and scale inhibition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao 266237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao 266237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen, phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) were synthesized by a low-cost and simple one-step hydrothermal method by using Amino trimethylene phosphonic acid (ATMP) and sodium citrate as the nitrogen source, phosphorus source and carbon source. The synthesized N, P-CQDs had bright blue fluorescence, good water dispersibility and stability. The fluorescence of N, P-CQDs was only quenched by Fe2+ and Fe3+, indicating extremely high selectivity to Fe2+ and Fe3+. The N, P-CQDs fluorescent probe could specifically measure Fe2+ and Fe3+ in the range of 0-600 μM and 0-250 μM and the limits of detection were 0.298 μM and 0.447 μM, respectively. The sensor system based on N, P-CQDs could also be used to determine Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in real water samples. Through research, it was found that the quenching mechanism of Fe2+/Fe3+ on N, P-CQDs may be static quenching. Besides, N, P-CQDs showed excellent CaCO3 scale inhibition efficiency. Therefore, synthesized N, P-CQDs has broad application prospects in iron ions detection and scale inhibition.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles, as a hot topic in the field of materials research this years, are widely used in pharmaceutical, biomedicine, and nanoscale etching, etc [1,2]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a category of excellent fluorescent nanoparticles, which has great research prospects. Since discovered in 2004 [3], they have been widely used in the fields of chemical and biological sensing [4,5], fluorescence tracing [6], bioimaging [7], photocatalysis [8], and biomedical materials [9] due to their high quantum yield, physical and chemical stability, excellent water solubility, and low toxicity [10,11]. Compared with the semiconductor quantum dots with high toxicity [12], low-cost and green synthetic CQDs have the opportunity to become more competitive fluorescent nanomaterials. Traditionally, the synthesis methods of CQDs are mainly divided into two types. The first is a top-down method, which breaks down macromolecular carbon precursor materials by laser ablation [13], ultrasonication [14], etc. The second is a bottom-up method, which synthesizes small molecular carbon sources into CQDs through electrochemical oxidation [15], hydrothermal [16], and microwave [17], and so on. And in the above methods, the hydrothermal synthesis method is considered one of the most common CQDs synthesis methods due to its advantages of simplicity, rapid, and low pollution emissions.

The doping of heteroatoms like boron, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur have been proved to affect the band gap and electron density of CQDs [18], which can increase their quantum yield and expand their application. Among these elements, nitrogen can be strongly combined with carbon atoms [19], and phosphorus atoms can be used as an n-type donor to optimize the electronic and optical properties of CQDs [20]. Therefore, the electronic properties of CQDs will be changed by N, P atoms doping, which makes them have unexpected properties. For example, N, P-CDs synthesized with 1,2-ethylenediamine, concentrated phosphoric acid, phthalic acid had a quantum yield of 15%, which were applied in Mn (Ⅶ) ions detection, temperature measurement and cellular imaging [21]. At present, the synthesis of N, P-doped carbon quantum dots mainly uses material containing phosphate groups and amino groups as precursors [18,22], which has great research prospects.

Iron element as one of the most essential trace elements in biosystems plays an important role in maintaining living systems [23]. Iron homeostasis disorders will cause an expansive range of diseases, like anemia, heart failure, intelligence decline, tissue inflammation, and cancer [24–26]. In recent years, the quenching or recovery of fluorescence of fluorescent materials has been applied in the field of detection [27], and CQDs as the new fluorescent materials also have the potential for selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+.For instance, Zhou et al. reported the NCQDs synthesized by ultrasonic treatment of dopamine, which showed sensitive quenching phenomenon toward Fe2+ ions and temperature-dependent fluorescence phenomenon [14]; Zhang et al. obtained N, P-CDs used maize starch via a solvothermal approach to effectively detected Fe3+ ions in water [24]. However, most of the CQDs synthesized by these studies only detected Fe2+ or Fe3+, and only two papers reported CQDs that could be quenched by both Fe2+ and Fe3+ [28,29]. Therefore, research on CQDs simultaneously detecting the two ions are very significative.

Amino trimethylene phosphonic acid (ATMP) is a typical scale inhibitor based on phosphoric acid [30], which can play the role of both nitrogen source and phosphorus source. Herein, we developed a facile one-step and cheap method for preparing N, P-CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of ATMP and sodium citrate. As-prepared N, P-CQDs displayed a spherical shape and excellent fluorescence characteristics. The blue fluorescence of N, P-CQDs had been selectively quenched by each Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions individually. The fluorescence sensor system based on N, P-CQDs was established to simultaneously detect Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, with LOD of 0.298 μM and 0.447 μM (lower than the standard value set by the U.S EPA). Then the reliability of the sensing system in real water samples was verified. In addition, the synthesized N, P-CQDs had high CaCO3 scale inhibition efficiency like ATMP.

ATMP (50 wt%) were provided by Shandong Taihe Water Treatment Technologies Co., Ltd (China). Trisodium citrate dihydrate was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). FeCl3•6H2O, MnSO4•H2O, CdCl2•2.5 H2O, FeSO4•7H2O, ZnCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2•6H2O, AgNO3, BaCl2•2H2O, CuSO4•5 H2O, NiSO4•6H2O, KCl and quinine sulfate were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (China). All solutions in the experiment were achieved with ultrapure water.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the N, P-CQDs were obtained on a transmission electron microscope (FEI, Tecnai G2 F20, American). The distribution of particle size was determined by nano measure software. The atom force microscope (AFM) images of the N, P-CQDs were obtained at tapping mode using an atomic force microscope (JPK, Nanowizard 4, Japan). The X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) of the N, P-CQDs were measured with an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Ultima IV, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of the N, P-CQDs were measured by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (TEI, K-Alpha+, American) with mono Alka source (energy:1486.6 eV). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the N, P-CQDs were obtained from an FT-IR spectrometer (TEI, Nicolet iS10, American). The absorption and fluorescence spectra of N, P-CQDs solution were measured on UV-vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2450, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-4600, Japan), respectively.

N, P-CQDs was prepared from ATMP and sodium citrate by the typical one-step hydrothermal method. First, ATMP (6.5 mmol) and sodium (13 mmol) citrate were dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water, the solution was then transferred into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave with polytetrafluoroethylene, heated it at 200°C for 5 h. The obtained yellow carbon dot solution was filtered with a 0.22 μM filtration membrane via a syringe filter, and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MW 500 Da) for 24 h to obtain an aqueous dispersion of N, P-CQDs. For subsequent characterization work, the N, P-CQDs solution was vacuum-dried for 24 h to obtain a yellow powder sample.

A previously established method [31] was used to calculate the fluorescence quantum yield of N, P-CQDs. Quinine sulfate (QY=54%) dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 was chosen as the reference substance. When the excitation light wavelength was 320 nm, the QY of N, P-CQDs was calculated as the following equation:
(1)
Y 1
=
Y 0
·
F 1
/
F 0
·
A 0
/
A 1
where Y1, Y0 are the QY of N, P-CQDs and reference, F1, F0 are the integrated fluorescence intensity of the N, P-CQDs and the reference, A1, A0 are the absorbance of the N, P-CQD and the reference, respectively. In this work, the absorbance of N, P-CQDs and reference at 320 nm was kept below 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions by N, P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As-prepared N, P-CQDs had a good fluorescence effect, exhibited blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light, and the fluorescence emission intensity showed a good linear versus its concentration (Fig. S1). In this work, N, P-CQDs solution was diluted with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH=7.2) to avoid pH fluctuation.

To investigate the selective detection of metal ions by N, P-CQDs, various metal ion solutions of 1 mM were prepared from corresponding salts, including Fe3+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+. A 5 mL of N, P-CQDs solution (100 μg mL−1) and 5 mL of metal ion solution (200 μM) was then added into a 10 mL colorimetric tube, and the solution was mixed and equilibrated for 10 min before spectral measurements. The sensitivity detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was confirmed similarly, 5 mL of N, P-CQDs solution (100 μg mL−1) was mixed with 5 mL of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ion solutions of a series of concentrations. A fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to record the fluorescence spectra of N, P-CQDs quenched by metal ions.

River water and tap water samples were collected from the campus of Shandong University (Shandong Qingdao). For removing suspended particles, the obtained water samples were filtered through 0.22 μM microporous membrane. The determined concentrations of Fe2+ and Fe3+ were added to 1 mL of above water sample, and then the spiked water sample was diluted to 10 mL with a PBS buffer solution containing N, P-CQDs. The fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured using 320 nm excitation after 10 min of equilibration.

The scale inhibition effect of N, P-CQDs and ATMP on CaCO3 was determined by Chinese national standard (GB/T 16632-2019, China). A testing solution with 240 mg L−1 Ca2+, 732 mg L−1 HCO3 −, 152 mg L−1 Na2B4O7 (buffer), and different concentrations of testing scale inhibitor was added to a 500 mL conical flask. The testing solution and the blank control group were immersed at 80°C for 10 h, and then filtered with filter paper. The Ca2+ concentration of the filtrate was measured by EDTA titration after the filtrate was cooled. The following equation was used to calculate the scale inhibition efficiency (η) of the testing scale inhibitor:
(2)
η =
ρ 2
−
ρ 1
ρ −
ρ 1
× 100 %
where ρ2, ρ1 are the Ca2+ concentration in the filtrate with and without scale inhibitors, respectively. ρ is the concentration of Ca2+ in the testing solution.

Blue fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorus-doped fluorescent CQDs were synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal approach using sodium citrate as carbon source, ATMP as nitrogen source and phosphorus source (Fig. 1 ). ATMP and sodium citrate were decomposed, condensed, neutralized, and finally carbonized to form N, P-CQDs. The main factors affecting the QY of the prepared N, P-CQDs were the ratio of reaction precursors, hydrothermal temperature and hydrothermal time. As shown in Fig. S2a, when the ratio of sodium citrate to ATMP was 1:2, prepared N, P -CQDs achieved the highest QY. Excessive or too little nitrogen and phosphorus doping would block the passivated surface defects, resulting in QY reduction. It can be seen from Fig. S2b that as temperature increased, the degree of carbonization of the synthesized product increased, and the QY of N, P-CQDs gradually increased. At 200°C, the QY of N, P-CQDs reached a maximum value, and then QY decreased with the increase of temperature. High temperature led to a decrease in QY of the product may be because the high temperature would cause oxidation and agglomeration of N, P-CQDs. In addition, the influence of hydrothermal reaction time on the QY of N, P-CQDs also was studied (Fig. S2c). The results clearly show that the increase of reaction time would have increased QY of N, P-CQDs. When the reaction time reached 5 h, continuing to increase the reaction time would not have a significant impact on QY. Therefore, the optimal reaction conditions were determined as follows: ratio of sodium citrate to ATMP was 1:2, the reaction temperature was 200°C, and the reaction time was 5 h. The QY of N, P-CQDs synthesized under this condition reached 17.74%, which is higher than most previously reported CQDs doped with N, P (Table S1 for details).

The morphology of prepared N, P-CQDs was observed by TEM and AFM. It can be seen in Fig. 2 a, TEM image shows that the synthesized N, P-CQDs were well dispersed and had a spherical shape. Statistics of the particle size of 100 dots shows the uniform size distribution of N, P-CQDs with an average particle size of 1.18 nm (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2b is the AFM image of the product, showing the height analysis result of N, P-CQDs. The height profile curve (inset in Fig. 2b) shows that the height distribution of N, P-CQDs is between 1.2 and 2.4 nm, which was consistent with TEM measurement results. According to the analysis results of TEM and AFM, the obtained N, P-CQDs display small size and relatively wide size distribution, which demonstrated the polydispersity of N, P-CQDs. The crystallinity of N, P-CQDs was determined by XRD. A broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ=25° was observed on the XRD pattern (Fig. 2d) of N, P-CQDs, which indicated the amorphous carbon of N, P-CQDs [32]. The interlayer spacing of 0.36 nm was calculated according to Bragg's law, which was higher than the (002) crystal lattice (0.34 nm). It showed that synthesized N, P-CQDs had a graphite lattice, and the increase in interlayer spacing may be due to the abundant functional groups on the surface. Moreover, the particle size of N, P-CQDs was calculated according to Scherer formula to be about 1.30 nm, which was consistent with TEM characterization results.

To discuss the chemical composition and state of the prepared N, P-CQDs, their XPS spectra were analyzed. It can be seen in Fig S3, the XPS survey spectrum of N, P-CQDs showed five characteristic peaks, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, P 2p, P 2s and O 1s, and the content of C, P, N, and O was 41.94%, 5.54%, 42.73%, and 9.78%, respectively. Fig. 3a shows that the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s of N, P-CQDs was divided into C=C (284.4 eV), C-C/C-N/C-P (285.3 eV), C-O (286.1 eV), C=O (287.7 eV) and O=C-O (288.5 eV) [20]. N, P-CQDs exhibited large C=C peaks and C-O/C=O peaks, which verified the sp2 graphitic C=C structure and high oxidation state of N, P-CQDs. The N 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3b) showed two peaks at 399.1 eV and 401.3 eV, corresponding to the existence of N-H and C-N/P-N bonds, respectively [33,34]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the P 2p spectrum of N, P-CQDs could be divided into the following two peaks: P-O bonds at 132.1 eV and P-C/P-N bonds at 133.0 eV [18]. In addition, the O 1s spectrum had four peaks at 530.3 eV, 531.8 eV, 535.1 eV, and 532.8 eV (Fig. S4), which could be attributed to C=O/P=O, C-O, C-OOH, and C-OH/P-OH, respectively [20,35]. The results of XPS indicated the successful doping of N and P elements in N, P-CQDs and proved the defects and disorders in the graphitized structure of N, P-CQDs, which was the main reason for the high QY. FT-IR spectrometry was used to compare the functional groups on the surface of prepared N, P-CQDs to its reaction precursors. It can be seen in Fig. 3d, FTIR spectra of the precursor and N, P-CQDs had many similar peaks, indicating that the groups in reaction precursor had successfully transferred into carbon dots. A wide absorption peak at 3324 cm−1 was attributed to O-H and N-H, which demonstrated the existence of hydroxyl and amino groups. The peak at 2958 cm−1 corresponded to C-H bond [36,37]. The characteristic peak at 1654 cm−1 resulted from the stretching vibrations of C=C, C=O in the conjugated structure [38]. The peaks at 1580 cm−1 and 1323cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N and bending vibration of N-H, which indicated that N atoms had been successfully doped into N, P-CQDs [39,40]. The peak at 1401 cm−1 was from COO- bond, confirming that synthesized N, P-CQDs had excellent water solubility [41]. Besides, there were sharp peaks at 1076 cm−1, 892 cm−1 and 550 cm−1, attributed to P=O, P-OH, and PO4 3− bonds, which indicated the existence of phosphorus-containing groups on the surface of N, P-CQDs [18,42,43]. Being compared with the spectrum of the reaction precursor, disappeared absorption peaks of N, P-CQDs at 2300 cm−1 and 1216 cm−1 showed that the reactants may be carbonized in hydrothermal process [44]. The result of FTIR spectra were consistent with XPS spectra, which further proved that N and P atoms are doped into the synthesized CQDs.

The optical properties of N, P-CQDs were characterized by UV-vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. In Fig. 4 a, the absorption spectrum of N, P-CQDs had two absorption peaks between 200 nm and 500 nm. The strong absorption peak at 232 nm could be attributed to the π-π transition of the conjugated C=C bond [45]. The weak absorption peak at 330 nm was due to the n-π transition of C=O bond and the excited defect surface states caused by doping of P, N atoms [46,47]. Moreover, the N, P-CQDs solution was light yellow under sunlight, and emitted bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 4a), corresponding to its excitation spectrum and emission spectrum. Furthermore, the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior was studied. As shown in Fig. 4b, when the excitation wavelength increased from 270 nm to 320 nm, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs gradually increased, and then decreased with increasing the excitation wavelength. Therefore, when excitation wavelength was 320 nm, N, P-CQDs was obtained maximum emission intensity at 425 nm. Moreover, the red-shift with increasing emission wavelength was discovered, indicating the excitation-dependent PL emission of N, P-CQDs, which would due to the polydispersity and surface groups of them [48]. . The great polydispersity and abundant surface groups of N, P-CQDs would also increase the band gap, which improved the fluorescence performance of carbon dots [49].

Besides, the fluorescence stability of N, P-CQDs was also studied to prove its application prospects. First, pH stability of N, P-CQDs was studied, Fig. 4c illustrates that the fluorescence emission intensity of N, P-CQDs was significantly affected when the pH changed between 2 and 11, and it reached the highest fluorescence intensity under pH=8. Therefore, in the subsequent metal ion detection experiments, PBS buffer solution (pH=7.2) was used as the solvent to avoid the influence of pH on experimental results. Fig. 4d shows the results of the salt tolerance experiment of N, P-CQDs, even if KCl concentration reached 2 M, the emission intensity of N, P-CQDs remains stable. The photostability of N, P-CQDs was studied by exposing the N, P-CQDs solution to UV irradiation (365 nm). As shown in Fig. S5, the emission intensity of N, P-CQDs changed slightly during UV irradiation time, which confirms that synthesized N, P-CQDs had excellent photostability. In addition, the long-term stability of N, P-CQDs has been verified. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of synthesized N, P-CQDs had not changed significantly after 60 days. The above results demonstrated that N, P-CQDs had excellent fluorescence properties and good stability, and can be applied to sensing detection.

Since CQDs may be quenched by metal ions, the selective quenching of N, P-CQDs by various metal ions had been investigated. Common metal ions were used, including Fe3+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+, the PL spectroscopy of synthesized N, P-CQDs were analyzed in the presence of various ions with a concentration of 100 μM. It can be seen from Fig. 5 a, when Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions were present, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of N, P-CQDs was significantly affected, while the presence of other metal ions had a negligible fluorescence quenching effect on intensity. Fig. 5b clearly shows the luminescence of N, P-CQDs solutions containing different metal ions under the 365 nm UV lamp. Compared with the addition of other investigated metal ions, the fluorescence color of the carbon dot solution with Fe2+ and Fe3+ was significantly darker. Above results showed that N, P-CQDs have a much higher selectivity for detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ than other interfering metal ions.

In order to further study the specificity of N, P-CQDs for Fe2+ and Fe3+ detection, an anti-interference experiment for Fe2+ and Fe3+ detection was performed in the coexistence of other metal ions. As shown in Fig. S7a, b, in the presence of 100 μM interfering metal ions, after adding Fe2+ or Fe3+ ions (100 μM), the fluorescence intensity of the N, P-CQDs solutions would still be significantly quenched. Moreover, compared with the blank sample, the reduction of fluorescence intensity is consistent, which indicated the influence of coexisting interfering metal ions on detection is negligible. The stability of the detection was also tested. After Fe2+/Fe3+ was added, the fluorescence of N, P-CQDs was quickly quenched and remained stable after two hours (Fig. S8). Above results proved that N, P-CQDs can accurately and stably determine Fe2+/Fe3+ in water samples, which had the potential for further applications.

To further study the sensitivity of N, P-CQDs to Fe2+ and Fe3+, the fluorescence spectra of N, P-CQDs solutions (pH=7.2) was recorded adding Fe2+ and Fe3+ with a series of concentrations. It can be seen in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CQDs gradually decreased as Fe2+ increased from 0 to 600 μM, and finally decreased to 19.24% of original fluorescence intensity. When Fe3+ was added, the fluorescence spectrum of N, P-CQDs changed similarly to Fe2+, the fluorescence intensity decreased to 29.14% of original value with the concentration of Fe3+ reaching 400 μM (Fig. 6c). Fig. 6b shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of N, P-CQDs and the concentration of Fe2+, in the range of 0-200 μM, the linear relationship could be described by: F0/F=0.0045[Fe2+] +0.9583 (R2=0.9921), and in the range of 200-600 μM, the linear relationship was described by: F0/F=0.0085[Fe2+] +0.0982 (R2=0.9939). There was also a good linear relationship between the concentration of Fe3+ and the F0/F of N, P-CQDs, but the range was smaller than that of Fe2+, the linear relationship between them in the range of 0-250 μM could be described by: F0/F=0.003[Fe3+] +0.9447 (R2=0.9803) (Fig. 6d). In addition, the limit of detection was measured, results showed that the LOD of Fe2+ was 0.298 μM and the LOD of Fe3+ was 0.447 μM. Interestingly, N, P-CQDs probes had wider detection range and lower limit of Fe2+ detection compared to Fe3+, indicating that N, P-CQDs were more sensitive to Fe2+.

Moreover, the detection experiments of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in real water samples were also performed by adding 100 μM/150 μM Fe2+/Fe3+ to river water and tap water samples. The recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated to evaluate detection accuracy. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions at the two different concentration levels were between 96% and 106%, and the RSDs were lower than 6%, indicating that the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ method based on N, P-CQDs could be applied to real water sample.

The way of fluorescence quenching was generally considered to be static quenching or dynamic quenching. When static quenching or dynamic quenching process occurred, the relationship between the degree of quenching and the concentration of quencher followed a typical Stern-Volmer equation.

F0/F=1+Ksv[Q] (3) where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity before and after quenching, Ksv is Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. In this study, there was an excellent linear relationship between the F0/F of N, P-CQDs and the concentration of Fe2+/Fe3+ in the presence of 0-600 μM Fe2+ or 0-250 μM Fe3+, which proved the quenching mechanism of Fe2+/Fe3+ on N, P-CQDs was static quenching or dynamic quenching. To further investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, we measured and compared the fluorescence lifetime, UV-vis absorbance spectra of Fe2+/Fe3+-N, P-CQDs system. The results of the fluorescence decay traces are shown in Fig. 7 a. The fluorescence lifetime of N, P-CQDs, Fe2+- N, P-CQDs, Fe3+ -N, P-CQDs were calculated to be 9.82 ns, 9.26 ns, and 9.28 ns (inset in Fig. 7a), which did not show obvious change. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime remained constant, and the quenching process was considered to be static quenching [31]. Fig. 7b shows the effect of adding Fe2+/Fe3+ on the absorption spectra of N, P-CQDs solution. The absorption spectra of N, P-CQDs changed significantly after adding Fe2+/Fe3+, that was the absorption at 330nm disappeared, indicating that new non-fluorescent complex was formed, which was consistent with the characteristics of static quenching [50]. In order to prove this conjecture, we also studied the effect of temperature on quenching efficiency. As shown in Fig. 7c, d, when the temperature changed from 20°C to 60°C, the quenching ratio of Fe2+/Fe3+ to N, P-CQDs was higher, which was a typical feature of static quenching [51]. Based on the above experimental results, the mechanism of Fe2+/Fe3+ quenching N, P-CQDs was Fe2+ or Fe3+ combined with the plentiful amino groups and P/O functional groups on the surface of N, P-CQDs to form non-fluorescent complex, resulting in the concentration of fluorescent N, P-CQDs reducing, which cause the static quenching of fluorescence. As for the difference between the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+, it was speculated that Fe2+ was more likely to form non-fluorescent complex with N, P-CQDs, while Fe3+ had fewer surface groups binding sites with N, P-CQDs.

Since the main synthetic precursor ATMP was a typical scale inhibitor and had been verified to effectively inhibit the deposition of CaCO3 [52]. Therefore, we speculated N, P-CQDs synthesized with ATMP as one of the raw materials also had the CaCO3 scale inhibition. The static scale inhibition efficiency of reaction precursors and N, P-CQDs on CaCO3 were measured, and the result is shown in Figure S9. The results showed that as the amount of N, P-CQDs increased, the scale inhibition efficiency increased. When the concentration of N, P-CQDs reached 100mg, the scale inhibition efficiency could reach about 90% at 80°C. In addition, compared with the reaction precursor, the scale inhibition effect of N, P-CQDs was not only retained, but even slightly better than before the reaction when it was added at low concentration. A possible scale inhibition mechanism was ATMP components and carboxyl groups on the surface of N, P-CQDs chelate with Ca2+ ions, which hampered the growth of inorganic salt crystals, reducing the formation of salt scale [52,53]. In addition, according to the experimental results in 3.4, Ca2+ did not quench the fluorescence of N, P-CQDs, considered that the chelation effect of N, P-CQDs on metal ions did not affect their fluorescence properties. Taken together, N, P-CQDs had the potential to become high-quality scale inhibitors, and the research of CQDs in scale inhibition was valuable.

In short, the N, P-CQDs with blue fluorescence were synthesized using sodium citrate as carbon source, ATMP as nitrogen source and phosphorus source via a facile one-step hydrothermal approach. Synthesized N, P-CQDs doped with N, P had a quantum yield of up to 17.74%, and displayed excellent salt tolerance and photostability. The N, P-CQDs could be applied as a fluorescent probe for both Fe2+ and Fe3+ detection with good selectivity and specificity. The detection method had a good linear relationship to Fe2+ and Fe3+ in the range of 0-600 μM and 0-250 μM, respectively. Even in real water samples, the detection system still had excellent feasibility. Furthermore, N, P-CQDs were verified to have excellent scale inhibition effects on CaCO3. Therefore, this N, P-CQDs are extremely promising in the field of metal ion detection and scale inhibition.

Shu-Rong Zhang: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shao-Kang Cai: Writing – review & editing, Software. Gui-Qiao Wang: Investigation. Jin-Zhi Cui: Validation. Can-Zhu Gao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2021.131173.
